div.div: · two concentric circles drawn on a disc

Draw two concentric circles on a round disc — one large, one small. Roll the disc along a flat surface for exactly one full rotation, marking where each circle starts and ends. Paradox: the larger outer circle travels a distance equal to its full circumference. But the smaller inner circle appears to travel the same distance — even though its circumference is shorter. How? The resolution: the smaller circle isn't purely rolling — it's also sliding slightly. This 'slip' makes up the difference. The paradox baffled philosophers for 2,000 years.

Aristotle's Wheel Paradox appears in the ancient Greek text 'Mechanica' and was seriously debated by mathematicians including Galileo (who proposed it might prove the existence of infinitesimals) until the development of infinitesimal calculus finally resolved it.

A wheel with two concentric circles of different sizes rolls along a surface. The smaller inner circle appears to cover the same distance in one rotation as the larger outer circle — even though their circumferences are different. This is Aristotle's Paradox, which puzzled philosophers for 2,000 years.

svg.text: Source: Puzzles Old and New (1893) · Heritage Parlor
